---
title: "Group Reflection"
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Skills inventory

For each skill area, describe what your group actually used it for. If
you did not use a skill, say so and note why.

### Functions & refactoring (W2)

*What we did:*

- function for plotting sample size distributions

- function for calculating weighted averages for aggregating data

- function for calculating variance using a given confidence interval

- functions for calculating lower/upper confidence interval limit

*What worked well:*

- using the function in the code once it was written validating function
  input

*What was hard:*

- had to do some research on stack overflow on how to pass the name of a
  variable to a function (eval(sym()))

### Debugging (W3)

*What we did:*

- Look at error messages and - if code ran without errors - look at
  outputs to check plausability

*What worked well:*

- First thinking about it myself, then using google and - if nothing
  else worked - ask AI

- benefiting from sites like stack-flow and slides from the previous
  course

- simplifying the code by dividing it and using test data to see where
  the problem is

- looking at the architecture of the functions and example uses in the
  Help function in RStudio

*What was hard:*

- using methods like backtrace to see where an error was

### Version control & remotes (W4)

*What we did:*

- pulling at the start of each session

*What worked well:*

*What was hard:*

- connecting local and remote (when an SSH-key is used for another git
  repo it can not be saved in general settings again)

### Quarto websites & collaborative coding (W5)

*What we did:*

- peer-reviews, assisting each other where necessary, merging each
  others branches

- worked on separate branches named after each of us

- didn't work on main directly

*What worked well:*

- distributing tasks about equally - I feel like we both contributed
  about the same keeping an over

- barely any merge conflicts and no nasty ones communicating when a
  merge conflict happened

- keeping an overview over the branches

*What was hard:*

- keeping commits small

- time management - we both did our task *very* close to respective
  deadlines.

### R packages (W6)

*What we did:*

- use imports as to not code pre-existing functionality

*What worked well:*

- rather clean code

*What was hard:*

- we had no formal package management but since we did not use that many
  packages I feel like that is okay, also most packages were standard so
  when people pull our project most users should not experience too many
  problems

### Initial data analysis & data cleaning (W7)

*What we did:*

- looking at missing values

- creating a new column for the date

*What worked well:*

- looking at the documentation and inferring why values might be missing

- filtering data irrelevant to our analysis

*What was hard:*

- getting a total overview over all indicators the data includes as it
  is very comprehensive but not structured to capture that at a quick
  glance

- getting an overview over variables without making to many decisions
  (filter out specific group? age-standardized or not?)

### Reproducibility, open data & renv (W9)

*What we did:*

*What worked well:*

*What was hard:*

### Analysis & inference strategies (W10)

*What we did:*

- for research question 2: first I calculated the variance of each
  observation from the given confidence intervals and then aggregated
  the variances for each year and calculated a mean value and confidence
  interval for each year, then I checked whether the confidence interval
  overlapped as a conservative estimate

*What worked well:*

*What was hard:*

- the data was only available for population chunks, never for
  individuals; as the data contains sensitive observation the reason is
  probably to ensure anonymity, but in consequence we couldn't apply the
  usual statistical techniques which require information about the
  distribution of values

- for research question 2: I realized afterwards, that I simply
  aggregated the variances from different sub-population group (e.g.
  gender, age), to calculate an aggregated variance, but I didn't take
  into account that the sub-populations differ from each other therefore
  underestimating the variance

### Communication & visualisation (W11)

*What we did:*

*What worked well:*

*What was hard:*

## Key analytical decisions

Describe 2–3 decisions that had a meaningful effect on your results or
workflow.

### Decision 1: \[short title\]

*What were we deciding between?*

*How did we reason through it?*

*In hindsight, would we decide differently?*

### Decision 2: \[short title\]

*What were we deciding between?*

*How did we reason through it?*

*In hindsight, would we decide differently?*

## Workflow & collaboration

*How did we divide work across group members?*

*What branching or Git workflow did we actually use — and did it match
the proposal?*

*What would we change about how we coordinated?*

## LLM usage

Describe up to 5 significant uses of an LLM tool during the project.

### Usage 1: \[task\]

- **Tool / model:**
- **What it produced:**
- **What we had to correct or verify:**

### Usage 2: \[task\]

- **Tool / model:**
- **What it produced:**
- **What we had to correct or verify:**
